What is a transparency log?
A transparency log is a Merkle tree.
Stop me if you've heard this one.
It's a pen-only Merkle tree, too.
It's not a Merkle search tree.
It's just a Merkle tree that keeps growing of entries.
And the entries are things that you want to have a list of visible to everybody and where
everybody has the same list of things.
That is a very useful primitive because, for example, think about package management.
I'm involved in Go and usually how package management works is that you have a registry
and you make an account, you upload a thing, but that's very centralized.
Go has a completely different thing.
It has a decentralized system where the name of the module is where you fetch it and there's
a mechanism for fetching that data.
Stop me if you've heard this one.
But the problem with that is that then you have problems like left pad, you have the problem
that if you connect to that website, it knows that you connected to it and that you're using
it, which is not great.
You can fix those things with a centralized system because you can put something that saves
those modules and keeps them available.
So sometimes having a centralized relay, stop me if you've heard this one, is useful.
However, the problem is that now you need to trust that centralized party, just like you
needed to trust PyPI and NPM.
Well, that's what we use transparency logs for.
There's a transparency log called the Go Jackson database, which is just a long list of every
Go module version and its hash.
It's append only, everybody sees the same list, and a client will only install a Go module
if it has a cryptographic proof of inclusion in that list.
This already works.
You are using this if you're developing in Go.
And a lot of people don't know because that's the beauty of tlogs, that you can build them
on top of a centralized experience, and they just work.
Python has tried so many times to get package outers to sign their thing, and it never works
because either people don't check the signature, or if people check the signature, now you're

In tlogs one day we turn them on.
And that works, and that's what the system database is.
This is derived from certificate transparency, which is what Sunlight was about, and there's
links later.
The point of this is that you can use transparency logs to provide accountability where it's
hard to provide decentralized trust.
Instead of saying we're going to make a decentralized system where you have to connect to different
